name: Meat Stock (Broth)
recipeIngredient: Beef bones: Trim fresh beef bones and place in a large stockpot or kettle, cover with water, add lid, and simmer for 3 to 4 hours. Remove bones, cool broth, and strain out meat. Skim off any fat and return the strained meat to the broth.

Chicken or turkey bones: Place large carcass bones in a large stockpot, cover with cold water, bring to boil, then reduce heat to maintain a simmer for 30 to 45 minutes. Remove bones, cool broth, and strain out meat. Discard excess fat and return the strained meat to the broth.
recipeInstructions:
1. Rinse beef bones under cold water.
2. Place beef bones in a large stockpot or kettle with enough cold water to cover by at least 1 inch.
3. Add lid to pot and bring mixture to a boil over high heat.
4. Reduce heat to maintain a simmer for 3 to 4 hours, skimming off any foam or fat that rises to the surface.
5. Remove bones from the broth using tongs or a slotted spoon, let cool slightly, then discard bones.
6. Strain the cooled broth through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container, pressing on solids to extract as much liquid as possible.
7. Skim off any remaining fat that floats to the surface of the strained broth.
8. Return the extracted meat and strained fat back to the broth in the stockpot or kettle.
9. Reheat the mixture over medium heat until it reaches a rolling boil, then transfer into clean jars leaving 1-inch headspace.
10. Wipe rims of jars clean, adjust lids, and process according to Table 1 or Table 2 based on the canning method used.
Table 1: Recommended processing time for Meat Stock in a dial-gauge pressure canner
Style of Pack: Hot
Jar Size: Pints, Quarts
Process Time: 20 minutes for Pints, 25 minutes for Quarts
Canner Pressure (PSI) at Altitudes of 0 - 2,000 ft: 11 lb.
2,001 - 4,000 ft: 12 lb.
4,001 - 6,000 ft: 13 lb.
6,001 - 8,000 ft: 14 lb.
Table 2: Recommended processing time for Meat Stock in a weighted-gauge pressure canner
Style of Pack: Hot
Jar Size: Pints, Quarts
Process Time: 20 minutes for Pints, 25 minutes for Quarts
Canner Pressure (PSI) at Altitudes of 0 - 1,000 ft: 10 lb.
Above 1,000 ft: 15 lb.
